title: Debt, EMIs & Credit Card Traps
concept: Good vs bad debt, EMIs, credit cards, BNPL
summary: Borrowing brings future money into the present — at a cost. EMIs and 'pay later' offers make wants feel affordable while quietly committing your future income. High-interest debt like credit-card balances is the most dangerous.

sections.heading: What debt really does
sections.body: A loan lets you buy now and pay later, plus interest. For an appreciating asset or a genuine need it can make sense; for a depreciating want it usually doesn't.

sections.heading: The EMI illusion
sections.body: A 'small' monthly EMI hides the full price and the interest. Several EMIs stacked together can lock up a big share of your salary before you've spent on anything else.

sections.heading: The credit-card trap
sections.body: Credit cards are great if you pay the full bill every month. Paying only the 'minimum due' carries the rest at very high interest (often ~36% a year) — one of the fastest ways to fall into a debt spiral.

quiz.prompt: Paying only the 'minimum due' on a credit card means:
options: The rest carries at very high interest

quiz.prompt: Borrowing usually makes the least sense for:
options: A depreciating want like a fancier phone
An emergency you can't otherwise cover
An asset that may grow in value

quiz.prompt: The 'EMI illusion' refers to how a small monthly EMI:
options: Hides the full price and the interest you pay
Reduces the total cost of an item

quiz.prompt: Roughly what annual interest can an unpaid credit-card balance carry?
What is the main risk of stacking several EMIs together?
options: They lock up a big share of your salary before you've spent on anything else
They improve your monthly cash flow
They reduce the total interest you pay

disclaimer: Educational content only. This is a simulation, not financial advice.
